prompt: Document the first-100-customer GO-TO-MARKET motion of comparable TRUST / ATTESTATION / compliance-proof products, with specific tactics and numbers. For each of Vanta, Drata, Secureframe, SafeBase, Sprinto, Oneleet, Delve, and any AI-trust-center startup: how did they get their first ~100 paying customers (channel, ICP, founder-led vs sales-led), how long did it take, what was the wedge price, and what single move inflected growth. Pull cited specifics: First Round / Lenny's / SaaStr interviews, founder X threads, YC pages (@handle+date or URL). Extract the repeatable PLAYBOOK steps that apply to kolm's $750 wedge into AI-native startups, ranked by transferability. Explicitly note where a tactic depended on conditions kolm lacks (timing, capital, integrations). Flag confidence per company; say which stories are well-documented vs. thin.
content:
**Vanta (YC W18, founded 2018 by Christina Cacioppo) has the best-documented early GTM among the group.**[[1]](https://review.firstround.com/podcast/how-vantas-founder-bet-big-on-startup-security-and-found-product-market-fit-christina-cacioppo/)[[2]](https://review.firstround.com/vantas-path-to-product-market-fit/)

- **ICP and early customers**: Primarily early-stage startups (e.g., Segment, Front, Lattice as very early wins via YC network; later Figma insight). Focus on those needing SOC 2 (or similar) to close enterprise deals. Founder discovery emphasized calendars and daily workflows of security/product teams at startups.[[2]](https://review.firstround.com/vantas-path-to-product-market-fit/)
- **Channel and motion**: Heavily founder-led sales via customer interviews and word-of-mouth/referrals. No real website or marketing hire until late 2020; signed first ~600 customers this way. Christina personally sold the first ~$500k ARR through direct outreach. YC network was a key wedge for initial traction. Shifted later to more structured sales (e.g., Stevie Case as CRO in 2021 when ARR was sub-$20M).[[3]](https://www.unusual.vc/how-vanta-found-product-market-fit-christina-cacioppo-on-startup-compliance/)[[4]](https://thegtmnewsletter.substack.com/p/gtm-158-from-startup-to-4b-unicorn-4fa)
- **Timeline to ~100 customers**: Roughly 2018–early 2019 window (first customer milestone internally noted around 4 months in one interview; scaled to hundreds quickly via referrals). First $500k sold founder-to-founder.[[5]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jScpHy4UNl4)
- **Wedge price**: Not explicitly detailed in sources, but positioned as accessible annual subscriptions for startups (upfront annual billing emphasized; one salesperson later eliminated monthly options to speed closes). Early deals were founder-negotiated.[[6]](https://www.saastr.com/the-early-days-5-things-vanta-got-right-and-5-it-got-wrong-getting-to-first-10m-arr/)
- **Inflection move**: Deep customer discovery leading to the insight that startups needed SOC 2 proof to sell to enterprises (Figma questionnaire story was pivotal); combined with relentless founder-led selling and word-of-mouth in the YC/startup ecosystem. Later Trust Center and automation features helped scale.[[2]](https://review.firstround.com/vantas-path-to-product-market-fit/)
- **Confidence**: High—multiple detailed First Round interviews, SaaStr mentions, founder posts, and YC context provide specifics on tactics, numbers, and motion.[[1]](https://review.firstround.com/podcast/how-vantas-founder-bet-big-on-startup-security-and-found-product-market-fit-christina-cacioppo/)[[2]](https://review.firstround.com/vantas-path-to-product-market-fit/)

**Drata (founded ~2020 by Adam Markowitz, ex-Portfolium/NASA)** has solid but less granular public details on the absolute earliest phase.[[7]](https://drata.com/blog/celebrating-two-years)

- **ICP and early customers**: Startups and growing companies needing automated SOC 2/ISO 27001 compliance and continuous monitoring. Beta customers helped validate.
- **Channel and motion**: Launched with strong beta trust from founding team background. First 100 customers secured in ~45 days post-launch via trust/referrals and product-led elements. Later scaled with sales structure.
- **Timeline**: Very rapid post-launch (45 days to 100 customers); reached thousands quickly.
- **Wedge price**: Not specified in results; positioned competitively for automation.
- **Inflection move**: Automation-first approach and team credibility for quick early traction; focus on “autopilot” compliance.
- **Confidence**: Medium—Drata’s own blog and interviews (e.g., Intercom, SaaStr) confirm the 45-day stat and beta-to-scale path, but fewer founder-led tactical specifics than Vanta.[[7]](https://drata.com/blog/celebrating-two-years)

**Secureframe (founded by Shrav Mehta)**: Limited public tactical details on first 100. Positioned as “TurboTax for security compliance” with upmarket sales motion. Early customers included investors; SaaStr interviews exist but focus more on later growth/AI features than precise early GTM numbers or timelines. ICP overlaps (startups to enterprises for SOC 2 automation). Confidence: Low/thin on specifics.[[8]](https://sacra.com/research/shrav-mehta-secureframe-turbotax-for-security-compliance/)

**SafeBase (founded by Al Yang; acquired by Drata ~2025)**: Trust Center-focused (proactive sharing of security posture to deflect questionnaires and accelerate sales cycles). Targeted organizations wanting customer-facing trust hubs. Early motion emphasized sales cycle reduction via self-serve trust materials. Limited granular numbers on first 100 or exact timeline/pricing in results. ICP: B2B SaaS with security review friction. Confidence: Low/thin (acquisition announcements and general interviews, but sparse early GTM docs).[[9]](https://drata.com/blog/acquiring-safebase)

**Sprinto, Oneleet, Delve**: Thin documentation overall. Sprinto targets cloud/SMB/startups with pre-built integrations and fast SOC 2 (e.g., claims of 6-day Type 1). Oneleet emphasizes “real” security/controls underneath compliance (founder background in pentesting). Delve faced allegations of fabricated reports in one public post (negative signal). No strong public interviews, founder threads, or numbers on first ~100 customers, timelines, pricing (~$10k–$40k range cited generically for similar tools), or inflections. Mostly comparison or customer stories. Confidence: Low/very thin across the board.[[10]](https://sprinto.com/blog/sprinto-vs-oneleet/)

**Other AI-trust-center startups**: Vanta and SafeBase (pre-acquisition) are the primary examples with Trust Center products. Broader AI-trust plays (e.g., questionnaire automation or AI-specific frameworks like NIST AI RMF) often build on the same SOC 2 wedge but add AI risk layers. Few have deep public early-GTM stories beyond the above. Vanta notably serves many Forbes AI 50 companies.[[11]](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/ccacioppo_it-took-us-two-years-to-grow-from-10mm-to-activity-7455252513089380353-qcYv)

**Repeatable PLAYBOOK steps (ranked by transferability to kolm’s $750 wedge into AI-native startups)**:
1. **Founder-led discovery + ICP validation via direct outreach** (highest transferability): Talk to 50–100+ potential users about calendars/workflows before building. Validate the exact pain (e.g., proving AI security posture to buyers/investors). Vanta’s calendar trick and Figma insight are highly replicable at low cost.
2. **Wedge on a painful, verifiable proof point (SOC 2 or equivalent readiness report)**: Start narrow (e.g., kolm’s $750 signed AI security audit/readiness report) to get quick wins and references. Annual upfront billing speeds cash and commitment.
3. **Leverage networks for first 20–50 customers** (YC, founder communities, AI startup Slack/Discord): Warm intros beat cold outreach. Vanta’s YC edge is timing-specific but network effects are transferable.
4. **Word-of-mouth/referral flywheel before marketing/sales hires**: Prioritize delighting early users for referrals. Vanta hit 600 customers this way—works especially well in tight-knit AI founder circles.
5. **Self-serve or low-friction entry for wedge pricing**: $750 price point enables product-led or founder-close motion without heavy sales overhead.
6. **Inflection via visible proof + category creation** (e.g., Trust Center equivalent for AI trust posture): Once you have 50–100 logos, productize sharing/proof to accelerate buyer sales cycles.

**Where tactics depended on conditions kolm likely lacks**:
- **Timing/competition**: Vanta/Drata benefited from being early in a nascent category with few competitors; later entrants face copycats and established players.
- **Capital/fundraising**: Vanta operated near break-even for years and raised big later; many tactics (long founder sales cycles, no website) assume runway. kolm’s low wedge helps but scaling beyond 100 may need capital for integrations/sales.
- **Integrations/ecosystem**: Deep cloud/SaaS integrations (Vanta/Drata strengths) require dev resources or partnerships that a small team may lack initially.
- **Network effects**: YC or equivalent warm intros are powerful but not universally available; pure cold or content-led may be slower.

**Overall notes**: Vanta’s story is the clearest template and most transferable at the wedge stage. Others are thinner, relying more on product credibility or later sales motions. For kolm, prioritize founder outreach in AI-native communities, nail the $750 proof point for quick references, and build referral momentum before layering sales. Sources primarily from First Round reviews, founder interviews (SaaStr, etc.), company blogs, and targeted searches; X threads were not heavily surfaced in results but could supplement founder handles like @christinacaci.
